title: practical english usage - Part 56
content:
All the trouble was caused by your mother.

agents are mentioned in only about 20 per cent of passive clauses.) thing that does the action, or that causes what happens. (Note, however, that In a passive clause, we  usually use by to introduce the agent - the person or 58  by   + agent For active verb forms, ►    2.

He has called. (active - present perfect) He was called. (passive - simple past) He was calling. (active - past progressive) Compare: 2. Past participles are used in both passive verb forms and active perfect tenses.

1. Be is used in both passive verb forms and active progressive tenses.

Mistakes like these are not surprising, because: This exhibition will be visit 5 million people.

The play performed in the evening.

We are write to each other-in English-.

These houses build in wood.

She has put in prison for life.

We were questioning by the immigration officer.

I was very interesting in the lesson.

Students often confuse active and passive verb forms in English. Typical 4 confusing       forms but not She was having a bath. (           A bath was being had by her.) but not Your mother lacks tact. (          Tact is lacked by your mother. ) Sylvia.) but not Sylvia resembles a Greek goddess. (          A Greek goddess is resembled by but not   m My shoes don't fit me. (          I'  not fitted by any shoes.) Section Passives           6 grammar •  59  passive modal structures: It can be done tomorrow.

She gave her sister the car.

A. verb + indirect object + direct object possible.

refer to a person (indirect object) and a thing (direct object). Two   structures are followed by two objects, an 'indirect object’  and a 'direct object’ . These usually Many verbs, such as give, send, show, lend, pay, promise, refuse, tell, offer, can be 61 verbs     with    two    objects      in  the   passive Our  house was built in 1827.

The new school will be opened by the Prime Minister on May 25th.

to say Charlemagne got crowned. . .) The Emperor Charlemagne was crowned in the year 800. (It would be strange The  passive with be is preferred for longer, more deliberate, planned actions.

He got caught by the police speeding through the town centre.

My watch got broken while I was playing with the children.

I get paid on Fridays. She's always getting invited to parties.

one's control, and that have good or bad consequences.

used to talk about events that happen by accident, unexpectedly, or outside be + past participle. This structure is mostly used in an informal style. It is often Get + past participle can be used to make passive structures in the same way as 60 get    as  passive      auxiliary:     He  got    cau   ght.

Anna wants to be phoned by Dan. (not the same meaning) - Dan wants to phone Anna.

Cats like to be chased by dogs. (different and - of course - untrue) - Dogs like to chase cats.

sentence completely. Compare: to the subject, so that a change from active to passive changes the sense of the With most other verbs that are followed by infinitives, their meaning is attached Cats may get chased by dogs.

- Dogs may chase cats.

Adverbs shouldn't be put between the verb and the object.

- You shouldn't put adverbs between the verb and the object.

His theory could be understood by a child.

- A child could understand his theory.

structure from active to passive, for example, without affecting the meaning very usually 'spreads over' a whole clause. This means that one can change a modal An interesting, rather complicated point about modal verbs is that their meaning They may close the hospital. -> The hospital may be closed.

We can do it tomorrow. -> It can be done tomorrow.

Modal  structures can be passivised.

done      tomorrow.

59 passive      modal     structures:      It can    be grammar •  62  verbs with prepositions in the passive For more about prepositions at the ends of clauses, ►    209.                                                                            -> not I don't like to be shouted at. (     /  don't like-to be shouted;) Note the word order. The  preposition cannot be dropped.
